content.text:
Base directory for this skill: ~/.claude/skills/contributing-to-open-source

# Contributing to Open Source (the high-acceptance workflow)

## Overview

Modeled on a contributor with ~90 merged PRs across major OSS repos (mvanhorn). Core principle: **make the maintainer's accept decision trivially cheap** — fix what they already want fixed, keep the diff reviewable in one sitting, and do their diagnosis and risk assessment for them.

## Step 0 — Repo recon (before writing any code)

- Read CONTRIBUTING.md and the PR template (`.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md`).
- **Find the correct base branch.** Check recently *merged* PRs' base; check why recent PRs were *closed* (wrong branch, too big, no tests). Targeting the wrong branch gets PRs closed unread.
- Note conventions: conventional-commit titles? AI-disclosure checkbox? Maintainer's language (add a translated summary if the project is non-English-led)?

## The 7 rules

1. **Fix reported bugs, don't invent features.** Start from an existing open issue; write `Fixes #NNNN`. Check the issue has no open linked PR (look at its timeline for cross-references). Abandoned/closed PRs on an issue are an opportunity: learn why they were rejected, redo it right.
2. **Keep the diff surgical.** Target +30 to +230 lines, 1–4 files, one commit. If the fix wants to grow, split scope and say what's deliberately out of scope.
3. **Explain root cause, not just the change.** Description skeleton: *what the bug is → the mechanism causing it (quote the offending code) → what the fix changes → what is deliberately untouched*. Rule out competing theories from the issue thread if any exist.
4. **Fill the PR template completely and honestly.** Every section, every checkbox truthfully. Disclose AI assistance when asked. State gaps plainly ("could not run X locally because Y").
5. **Ship tests with the fix.** Add a unit test that fails before and passes after. Include a "How to verify" section: exact command + expected before/after behavior.
6. **Defer to maintainers instantly.** Rebase, retarget, rename, split — whatever they ask, do it the same day and say so at the top of the PR ("Re-opening #X against `dev` as requested by @maintainer").
7. **State risk and scope explicitly.** One line on what a regression would look like and how the tests would catch it. Explicit low risk = cheap merge.

## Quick reference

| Signal to check before starting | How |
|---|---|
| Issue unclaimed | `gh api repos/O/R/issues/N/timeline` → no open cross-referenced PR |
| Correct base branch | Base of recently merged PRs; maintainer comments on closed PRs |
| Maintainer merges outsiders | `gh api "search/issues?q=repo:O/R+type:pr+is:merged"` total_count vs contributor count |
| PR template sections | `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md` |

## Common mistakes

- Opening the PR against the default branch when the project develops on `dev`/`develop`.
- A perfect fix with an empty or auto-generated description — maintainers merge explanations, not diffs.
- Bundling drive-by refactors into a bugfix ("while I was here…") — it doubles review cost.
- Claiming "tested" without saying how; broken checkbox trust kills future PRs.


ARGUMENTS: Find the best repo to contribute to among repos mvanhorn has contributed to, optimizing for likelihood-of-acceptance to stars ratio

Done — I researched mvanhorn's merged-PR history (1,976 merged PRs across ~180 repos), scored the external repos on acceptance-likelihood vs. stars, and there's a clear winner.

## Recommendation: **aethersdr/AetherSDR**

It has by far the best acceptance-to-stars ratio of anything mvanhorn has contributed to:

| Signal | Value |
|---|---|
| Stars | **171** (smallest serious candidate) |
| PRs merged since June 1 | **300** — they merge multiple outsider PRs *per day* |
| Open issues | 286, including **21 labeled "good first issue"** |
| mvanhorn's merged PRs there | 4 (recent — proof they merge outsiders like you) |
| Activity | pushed today, C++ (software-defined radio) |

A 171-star repo merging 300 PRs a month is an almost frictionless acceptance pipeline — the maintainers are actively pulling in external fixes, and there's a deep pool of unclaimed issues to pick from. The only real consideration is that it's a C++ codebase in the SDR domain.

**Runners-up**, in case C++/radio isn't your thing:

1. **stefanhendriks/Dune-II---The-Maker** — 359 stars, 86 PRs merged last month, 392 open issues. A C++ remake of the Dune 2 game; fun domain, very merge-friendly, but no "good first issue" labels.
2. **MasterJ93/ATProtoKit** — 220 stars, 28 PRs merged last month, 12 open issues (4 good-first). Swift library for the AT Protocol (Bluesky); small and welcoming but fewer issues to choose from.
3. **terrakube-io/terrakube** — 918 stars, 43 merged last month, 109 open issues, 8 good-first. Java; solid but a worse ratio than the top three.

For contrast, the big-name repos in his history (open-notebook at 34k stars, CodeWhale at 39k, qwen-code at 25k) are exactly what the ratio penalizes — huge visibility, crowded issue trackers, much stiffer competition per issue.

When you're ready, say the word and I'll do Step 0 of the skill on AetherSDR (or a runner-up): read its CONTRIBUTING.md and PR template, find the correct base branch, and shortlist unclaimed issues with no open linked PR that fit the surgical-diff profile.

content.content:
{"assignee":null,"body":"## Follow-up from #3940 review

#3940 fixed #3939 by syncing the RC-28 wheel accumulator (`m_flexTargetMhz`) to the snapped frequency after the auto-snap timer fires. That resync sits **inside** the `if (std::abs(snapped - s->frequency()) > 1e-9)` guard in the auto-snap lambda ([`MainWindow_Controllers.cpp` ~2429-2432](https://github.com/aethersdr/AetherSDR/blob/main/src/gui/MainWindow_Controllers.cpp)), so it only runs when the snap actually *moves* the frequency.

There's an adjacent, **pre-existing** gap the current fix doesn't cover: if `m_flexTargetMhz` is active-but-stale while the slice has *already* been moved onto the 1 kHz grid by a different controller, the snap timer no-ops and the stale accumulator survives.

### Reproduction (mixed RC-28 + MIDI tune knob)

1. Turn the RC-28 wheel — coalesce applies it, `m_flexTargetMhz` = e.g. `5.099500` MHz (stays active; `IncrementalTune` doesn't reset it). The 600 ms auto-snap timer is armed.
2. **Before** the snap timer fires, nudge a MIDI tune knob so the slice lands exactly on `5.100000` MHz. The MIDI path drives its own `m_midiTuneTargetMhz` and calls `applyTuneRequest(..., IncrementalTune, "midi-relative")` — it never touches `m_flexTargetMhz`, and `IncrementalTune` is not one of the intents that reset it (only `CommandedTargetCenter`/`AbsoluteJump` do, `MainWindow.cpp:5856`).
3. The RC-28 auto-snap timer fires: `snapped == 5.100000 == frequency()`, so `|Δ| < 1e-9` → guard false → `m_flexTargetMhz` is **not** resynced (still `5.099500`).
4. Next RC-28 `+100 Hz` tick: the re-base guard sees drift `|5.099500 - 5.100000| = 500 Hz < 0.001` → does **not** re-base → accumulates from the stale base: `5.099500 + 0.000100 = 5.099600` MHz.

**Result:** the slice jumps ~400 Hz *backward* instead of `+100 Hz` forward.

### Notes

- This is **not a regression from #3940** — the same stale-accumulator-after-MIDI condition exists with or without that PR. #3940 simply didn't extend to cover it.
- Requires two incremental controllers in use (RC-28 + MIDI). Narrow, but real in a mixed shack.

### Suggested fix

Move `m_flexTargetMhz = snapped;` **outside** the `> 1e-9` guard so the accumulator resyncs whenever the auto-snap timer fires, regardless of whether the snap itself moved the frequency:

```cpp
const double snapped = std::round(s->frequency() * 1000.0) / 1000.0;
if (std::abs(snapped - s->frequency()) > 1e-9)
    applyTuneRequest(s, snapped, TuneIntent::IncrementalTune, "rc28-autosnap");
m_flexTargetMhz = snapped;   // resync even when already on-grid
```

Re-activating an otherwise-inactive (`-1.0`) accumulator to the current frequency is harmless — the re-base guard in `applyFlexControlWheelAction` already handles the active/current-freq case, and the coalesce timer is not started by the snap path.

### Acceptance

- Repro sequence above: the next RC-28 tick moves `+100 Hz` forward from `5.100000`, not backward.
- No regression on the #3939 sub-kHz-snap case or normal incremental wheel tuning.

TITLE: Frequency readout in VfoWidget and RxApplet freezes during direct entry, ignoring live frequency changes
LABELS: bug,GUI,maintainer-review
BODY: ### Report preparation

- [x] I used the AI-assisted bug report tool (Help → Support → File an Issue)
- [ ] I have attached a support bundle or log file

### What happened?

## What happened

The frequency readout in both the VfoWidget and the RxApplet stops tracking the live slice frequency as soon as direct frequency entry is started by double-clicking the digits. Until the entry is committed or cancelled, the visible digits stay frozen at the value captured when entry began, even though the underlying slice frequency has actually changed.

This is most clearly seen when the frequency is changed by a route that does not move keyboard focus away from the entry field:

- scrolling the mouse wheel over the panadapter, or
- a tune commanded by an external controller (for example a Maestro over FlexLib).

In those cases the panadapter VFO marker moves to the new frequency, but the selected digits in the readout still show the old value.

The freeze is independent per element: starting direct entry in the VfoWidget does not freeze the RxApplet readout, and vice versa. Each owns its own entry state.

Also observed: clicking elsewhere in the waterfall moves the VFO marker to the clicked frequency, yet the selected digits in the VfoWidget still show the old value. The focus-transfer mechanics of this particular path have not been traced, so it is reported as an observation rather than with a confirmed mechanism.

## Environment

- AetherSDR version: `v26.6.4`
- OS: Linux (CachyOS / 
=========== AetherSDR #3792 ===========
TITLE: Linux startup delayed ~60 seconds on GNOME when MQTT keychain access fails ("keychain read failed: Unknown error")
LABELS: bug,GUI,priority: medium,SmartLink,maintainer-review
BODY: ### Report preparation

- [x] I used the AI-assisted bug report tool (Help → Support → File an Issue)
- [ ] I have attached a support bundle or log file

### What happened?

### Title: Linux startup delayed ~60 seconds on GNOME when MQTT keychain access fails ("keychain read failed: Unknown error")

### What happened?

Since upgrading to AetherSDR 26.6.4, AetherSDR stalls during startup on Ubuntu 26.04 LTS for approximately one minute before the GUI appears.

When launching the application, no main window is visible and the application appears hung. After roughly 60 seconds, startup completes and the GUI is finally displayed.

The only notable message in the log during this delay is:

```text
[15:09:55.978] WRN aether.mqtt: MqttApplet: keychain read failed: "Unknown error"
```

The issue appears to be related to the MQTT credential storage changes introduced in 26.6.4. I have verified that the GNOME keychain is functioning normally and can be accessed through Seahorse without issue.

Based on the timing of the regression, I suspect this may be a side effect of PR #3640. The implementation appears to have been validated against KDE KWallet, but may not correctly handle GNOME Keyring / Secret Service environments. It appears that a failed keychain lookup may be triggering a blocking timeout during application startup rather than failing immediately and allowing the UI to continue loading.

This behavior was not observed in earlier AetherSDR releases.

### What did you expect?


=========== AetherSDR #3952 ===========
TITLE: CI: dialog-size regression tests (pan_layout / flex_control) are built but not registered with add_test, so ctest never runs them
LABELS: bug,awaiting-response
BODY: ## Follow-up from #3941 review

#3941 added `pan_layout_dialog_size_test` as a "new standalone regression test" guarding the New-Panadapter Cancel-button overlap. It's a solid test — but as wired it **never runs in CI**.

### Mechanism

- CI runs the suite via `ctest --test-dir build` (`.github/workflows/sanitizers.yml`), which only executes targets registered with `add_test()`.
- There are **54 `add_test()` registrations for 79 `*_test` executables** — 25 test binaries are built but never run.
- `pan_layout_dialog_size_test` is one of them (no `add_test()`, no direct invocation in `.github/` or `scripts/`). It inherits this from `flex_control_dialog_size_test` (#3662), which is *also* unregistered.

### Impact

Both dialog-size guards currently protect only **compilation**, not runtime behavior. A future change that re-introduces the Cancel/grid overlap (or the AetherControl min-height regression #3662 guards) would compile fine and CI would stay green — the tests silently protect nothing.

### Fix

Register both with ctest:

```cmake
add_test(NAME pan_layout_dialog_size_test COMMAND pan_layout_dialog_size_test)
add_test(NAME flex_control_dialog_size_test COMMAND flex_control_dialog_size_test)
```

Both construct a `QApplication` and call `dialog.show()`, so on a headless CI runner they need an offscreen platform — set `QT_QPA_PLATFORM=offscreen` in the test environment (via `set_tests_properties(... PROPERTIES ENVIRONMENT ...)` or the ctest env), matching however other QApp
=========== AetherSDR #3946 ===========
TITLE: CI: -RequirePdb throw is swallowed by continue-on-error, so a missing PDB doesn't fail the build
LABELS: bug,Windows,awaiting-response
BODY: ## Follow-up from #3934

#3934 added `-RequirePdb` to `create-msix.ps1` and passes it from the Windows Installer workflow, with the stated goal that **CI fails loudly if the PDB ever goes missing again** (instead of silently shipping a symbol-less `.msixupload` to Partner Center).

That goal is **not actually achieved**, because the guard's `throw` fires *inside* a step that tolerates failure.

### Mechanism

- `packaging/windows/create-msix.ps1 -RequirePdb` `throw`s when no PDB is found.
- But that runs in the **`Create MSIX package`** step of `.github/workflows/windows-installer.yml`, which is marked **`continue-on-error: true`** (intentionally, to tolerate SDK/packaging hiccups).
- `continue-on-error: true` converts the step's outcome to *success* for job status — so the `throw` is swallowed and **the job stays green**.

### Impact on a real PDB regression

If the PDB regresses (e.g. someone flips `CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE` back to `Release`, or the linker PDB path changes):

1. `create-msix.ps1 -RequirePdb` throws → `Create MSIX package` step "fails" → swallowed by `continue-on-error`.
2. No `.msixupload` / `.appxsym` is produced.
3. The `Upload artifacts` glob "may match nothing" (per its own comment) → uploads a partial/empty set.
4. The standalone `Upload debug symbols` step is skipped by its `if: hashFiles('build/AetherSDR.pdb') != ''` guard.
5. **CI is green.** The regression lands unnoticed — the exact silent-failure class #3934 set out to eliminate, just relocated from "sh

- `pan_layout_dialog_size_test` is one of them (no `add_test()`, no direct invocation in `.github/` or `scripts/`). It inherits this from `flex_control_dialog_size_test` (#3662), which is *also* unregistered.

### Impact

Both dialog-size guards currently protect only **compilation**, not runtime behavior. A future change that re-introduces the Cancel/grid overlap (or the AetherControl min-height regression #3662 guards) would compile fine and CI would stay green — the tests silently protect nothing.

### Fix

Register both with ctest:

```cmake
add_test(NAME pan_layout_dialog_size_test COMMAND pan_layout_dialog_size_test)
add_test(NAME flex_control_dialog_size_test COMMAND flex_control_dialog_size_test)
```

Both construct a `QApplication` and call `dialog.show()`, so on a headless CI runner they need an offscreen platform — set `QT_QPA_PLATFORM=offscreen` in the test environment (via `set_tests_properties(... PROPERTIES ENVIRONMENT ...)` or the ctest env), matching however other QApp

TITLE: CI: -RequirePdb throw is swallowed by continue-on-error, so a missing PDB doesn't fail the build
LABELS: bug,Windows,awaiting-response
BODY: ## Follow-up from #3934

#3934 added `-RequirePdb` to `create-msix.ps1` and passes it from the Windows Installer workflow, with the stated goal that **CI fails loudly if the PDB ever goes missing again** (instead of silently shipping a symbol-less `.msixupload` to Partner Center).

That goal is **not actually achieved**, because the guard's `throw` fires *inside* a step that tolerates failure.

### Mechanism

- `packaging/windows/create-msix.ps1 -RequirePdb` `throw`s when no PDB is found.
- But that runs in the **`Create MSIX package`** step of `.github/workflows/windows-installer.yml`, which is marked **`continue-on-error: true`** (intentionally, to tolerate SDK/packaging hiccups).
- `continue-on-error: true` converts the step's outcome to *success* for job status — so the `throw` is swallowed and **the job stays green**.

### Impact on a real PDB regression

If the PDB regresses (e.g. someone flips `CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE` back to `Release`, or the linker PDB path changes):

1. `create-msix.ps1 -RequirePdb` throws → `Create MSIX package` step "fails" → swallowed by `continue-on-error`.
2. No `.msixupload` / `.appxsym` is produced.
3. The `Upload artifacts` glob "may match nothing" (per its own comment) → uploads a partial/empty set.
4. The standalone `Upload debug symbols` step is skipped by its `if: hashFiles('build/AetherSDR.pdb') != ''` guard.
5. **CI is green.** The regression lands unnoticed — the exact silent-failure class #3934 set out to eliminate, just relocated from "sh

content.text:
Yes — found it. **AetherSDR issue [#3952](https://github.com/aethersdr/AetherSDR/issues/3952)** is the best mvanhorn-style target, and the repo itself checks every box.

## The repo: aethersdr/AetherSDR

- **16.6KB CONTRIBUTING.md** + PR template + `AGENTS.md`/`CLAUDE.md` — it explicitly recommends contributing with Claude Code and lets AI agents self-assign issues.
- Requirements are clear: branch from `main`, **signed commits** (branch protection enforces it), PR template asks for a test plan and to cite a "Constitution principle."
- 300 PRs merged in the past month on a 171-star repo, and mvanhorn has 4 merged PRs there.

## The issue: #3952 — "CI: dialog-size regression tests are built but not registered with add_test, so ctest never runs them"

Why it's the pick, rule by rule:

1. **Reported bug, not a feature** — labeled `bug`, and filed by the maintainer himself (ten9876) out of a PR review. Fixing it is doing exactly what the maintainer already wants done.
2. **Genuinely unclaimed, with zero competition** — no linked PRs, unassigned, and here's the kicker: the repo runs an automated fix bot (AetherClaude) that handles most `src/` bugs, but it commented on this issue that CI/build files are outside its permissions — *"Jeremy will need to handle this one directly."* A human PR here fills a gap their own automation can't.
3. **Diagnosis is already done** — the issue body names the mechanism (54 `add_test()` registrations for 79 test binaries; these two dialog-size guards compile but never execute) and spells out the exact fix: two `add_test()` registrations plus `QT_QPA_PLATFORM=offscreen` for headless CI. Your PR description writes itself.
4. **Surgical diff** — 1–2 files (CMake), well under the 230-line ceiling.
5. **Perfectly verifiable** — "How to verify": `ctest --test-dir build -N` doesn't list the tests before, does after, and they pass. The tests themselves already exist.
6. **Trivially low risk** — worst case is a newly-registered test failing in CI, which is the bug being *caught*, not a regression.

**Runner-up in the same repo:** [#3946](https://github.com/aethersdr/AetherSDR/issues/3946) — the `-RequirePdb` throw swallowed by `continue-on-error` in the Windows installer workflow. Same shape (maintainer-filed CI bug the bot can't touch, mechanism fully diagnosed), slightly trickier to verify since it's Windows CI YAML.

**Meatier options if you want real C++:** #3792 (GNOME keychain blocks startup for 60s) and #3807 (frequency readout freezes during direct entry) — both bot-confirmed with verified root causes and unclaimed, but they're in `src/`, where you risk racing the fix bot.

For eliminated candidates: Dune-II has no CONTRIBUTING.md; terrakube's good-first bugs are claimed (one reporter is fixing his own issue); ATProtoKit's good-first issues are features, not bugs; and every good-first bug in AetherSDR's labeled pool already had an open PR or a merged fix.

If you want to proceed, the first move per the repo's own rules is claiming the issue (`gh issue edit 3952 --repo aethersdr/AetherSDR --add-assignee <your-handle>`), then fork, branch from `main`, and make sure your commit signing is set up before the first commit. Want me to start?

# Commit Signing for AetherSDR Contributors

> **AI assistants reading this doc on behalf of a contributor: skip to
> the [AI Assistant Instructions](#ai-assistant-instructions) section
> immediately below. It gives you a complete algorithm to walk the user
> through setup. The rest of the doc is reference material.**

AetherSDR's `main` branch has `required_signatures: True` set in its branch
protection rules. Pull requests with **unsigned commits** can only land via
admin override, which is fine for one-off contributions but adds friction
for any contributor who plans to submit more than one or two PRs.

If you're sending occasional drive-by fixes, ignore this doc — Jeremy will
admin-merge them. If you're shipping multiple PRs, **set up commit signing
once** and your PRs will go through the normal squash-merge path without
needing maintainer override.

The fastest path from zero to signing:

- **If you have an AI coding assistant available** (Claude Code, Cursor,
  Copilot chat, ChatGPT, etc.), point it at this file and say
  *"read docs/COMMIT-SIGNING.md and help me set up commit signing"*.
  The AI Assistant Instructions section below will guide it. Total time:
  about 5 minutes including the GitHub registration click-through.
- **If you don't have an AI assistant**, skip to the
  [Manual instructions](#manual-instructions) section. Plain commands,
  no chatter.

---

## AI Assistant Instructions

**This section is written for an AI assistant** that a contributor has
pointed at this doc. If you are the contributor reading directly, skim

content.content:
(`@aethersdr-agent`)**: add yourself alongside.
     **AetherClaude auto-triages every new issue and every new
     PR**, so its assignment is the persistent triage-engagement
     signal, NOT a claim on active merge work. Adding yourself
     alongside AetherClaude is expected and correct.
   - **Already assigned to another human agent or AI agent**
     (not AetherClaude): leave a coordination comment instead of
     double-assigning, and do not proceed with overlapping work.
2. **While** working: stay assigned. Other agents will see the
   non-AetherClaude assignment and route around you.
3. **After** posting the comment / completing the review / merging:
   the assignment can stay. GitHub auto-clears assignees when an
   issue closes or a PR merges. Manual unassign is optional but
   appropriate when you've concluded but the issue/PR remains open
   (e.g., you reviewed and recommended merge but didn't merge
   yourself).
4. **If your work is interrupted** (token limit, context loss,
   model failure): leave a brief comment ("Stepping away;
   unassigning so another agent can pick up") and unassign. The
   claim is mortal — it dies with the agent that held it
   (Principle X). AetherClaude's assignment is separate and stays.
5. **Quick read-only actions don't require assignment**: pulling an
   issue's title to summarize, listing PRs in a status report,
   counting open issues. Assignment is for engagement that produces
   a comment, review, or merge.

**GitHub CLI command**:

```bash
# Assign yourself to issue NNNN or PR NNNN
gh issue edit NNNN --add-assignee @me
gh pr edit NNNN --add-assignee @me

# Unassign yourself
gh issue edit NNNN --remove-assignee @me
gh pr edit NNNN --remove-assignee @me

# Check current assignees
gh issue view NNNN --json assignees
gh pr view NNNN --json assignees
```

**Why this exists**: without a visible claim signal, two agents
working from different orchestrators or contributor IDEs can both
spend tokens reviewing the same PR, post conflicting recommendations
within minutes of each other, and waste the maintainer's review time
reconciling them. The assignees list is the cheap, persistent,
multi-agent-visible claim mechanism that prevents this. It is the
operational implementation of Principle X.

### Autonomous Agent Boundaries

AI agents (including AetherClaude/pi-claude) may autonomously fix:
- **Bugs with clear root cause** — persistence missing, guard missing, crash fix
- **Protocol compliance** — matching SmartSDR behavior confirmed by pcap/FlexLib
- **Build/CI fixes** — missing dependencies, platform compat

AI agents must **NOT** autonomously change:
- **Visual design** — colors, fonts, layout, theme (user preferences ≠ project direction)
- **UX behavior** — how controls work, what clicks do, keyboard shortcuts
- **Architecture** — adding new threads, changing signal routing, new dependencies
- **Feature scope** — adding features beyond what the issue describes
- **Default values** — changing defaults that affect all users based on one report

When in doubt, the agent should implement the fix and note in the PR that
design decisions need maintainer review. The project maintainer (Jeremy/KK7GWY)
is the sole authority on visual design and UX direction.

## C++ Style Guide

- **No `goto`** — use early returns, break, or restructure the logic
- **No raw `new`/`delete`** — use `std::unique_ptr`, `std::make_unique`, or Qt parent ownership
- **No `#define` macros for constants** — use `constexpr` or `static constexpr`
- **Braces on all control flow** — even single-line `if`/`else`/`for`/`while`
- **`auto` sparingly** — use explicit types unless the type is obvious from context (e.g. `auto* ptr = new Foo` is fine, `auto x = foo()` is not)
- **Naming**: classes `PascalCase`, methods/variables `camelCase`, constants `kPascalCase`, member variables `m_camelCase`
- **Platform guards**: prefer `Q_OS_WIN` / `Q_OS_MAC` / `Q_OS_LINUX` for new code. Existing `_WIN32`/`__APPLE__` guards can be migrated opportunistically — don't do a blanket rewrite.
- **Don't remove code you didn't add** — if rebasing, ensure upstream changes are preserved. Review the diff before submitting.
- **Atomic parameters for cross-thread DSP** — main thread writes via `std::atomic`, audio thread reads. Never hold a mutex in the audio callback for parameter updates.
- **Error handling**: log with `qCWarning(lcCategory)`, don't throw exceptions

## Build

```bash
cmake -B build -G Ninja -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=RelWithDebInfo
cmake --build build -j$(nproc)
./build/AetherSDR
```

Full dependency list is in `README.md` — don't duplicate it here.

Current version: **26.7.1** (set in both `CMakeLists.txt` and `README.md`).
Versioning scheme is **CalVer** (`YY.M.patch[.hotfix]`) starting from v26.5.1,
the 1.0-equivalent. Hotfix sub-patches use a 4th component (e.g. 26.5.2.1).
Earlier tags used semver through v0.9.8.

---

## CI/CD Workflow

CI runs in Docker image `ghcr.io/ten9876/aethersdr-ci:latest` (~3.5 min builds).
**If you add a new `find_package(...)` to CMakeLists.txt, also add the
corresponding `-dev` package to `.github/docker/Dockerfile` and push.** The
`docker-ci-image.yml` workflow rebuilds the image automatically (~3 min); wait
for that before the next CI run can use it.

**`git ship`** alias — squashes local commits ahead of origin/main, creates a
branch, pushes, opens a PR with auto-squash-merge enabled. Commit freely
locally, then ship once.

Branch protection: signed commits required on main, CI must pass, CODEOWNERS
review required, branches auto-delete after merge.

**Helping a contributor set up commit signing?** Read
`docs/COMMIT-SIGNING.md` — the top of that file has explicit
AI-assistant instructions (algorithm, anti-patterns, completion
message). Works for Windows / macOS / Linux / WSL / Raspberry Pi
contributors. Default to SSH signing; GPG is the fallback for
contributors with existing GPG workflows.

=== sanitizers.yml test step ===
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@9c091bb21b7c1c1d1991bb908d89e4e9dddfe3e0

      - name: Configure
        env:
          CXXFLAGS: ${{ matrix.sanitizer.extra_cxx }}
          LDFLAGS:  ${{ matrix.sanitizer.extra_ld }}
        run: |
          cmake -B build -G Ninja \
            -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=${{ matrix.sanitizer.build_type }}

      - name: Build
        run: cmake --build build --parallel

      - name: Run tests under sanitizer
        id: ctest
        continue-on-error: true
        env:
          QT_QPA_PLATFORM: offscreen
        # The container image's /bin/sh is Dash, which doesn't understand
        # `set -o pipefail` or ${PIPESTATUS[0]} — without `shell: bash`
        # the step exits at code 2 before reaching ctest, the
        # continue-on-error swallows it, and downstream gating on
        # `outputs.exit` sees an empty string.  Net result: every run
        # appears green and no tests actually execute under the
        # sanitizers.  Pin to bash so the bashisms work as intended.
        #
        # `|| ec=$?` is also required: GitHub Actions' default bash flags
        # include `-e`, so a non-zero pipeline exit (e.g. ctest reporting
        # a test failure or sanitizer crash) would terminate the script
        # before the `echo "exit=..."` line wrote outputs.exit.  Without
        # outputs.exit set, the downstream `if: outputs.exit != '0'`
        # gates all skip — the very failure path we wrote them for.
        # Capturing into `ec` survives -e and lets us always emit the
        # real ctest exit code.
        shell: bash
        run: |
          set -o pipefail
          # Export each KEY=value line from runtime_env into the test env.
          while IFS= read -r line; do
            [ -n "$line" ] && export "$line"
          done <<'EOF'
          ${{ matrix.sanitizer.runtime_env }}
          EOF
          ec=0
          ctest --test-dir build --output-on-failure 2>&1 | tee sanitizer.log || ec=$?
          echo "exit=$ec" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Extract failure report
        id: extract
        if: steps.ctest.outputs.exit != '0'

content.content:
# Claude Code Project Guide for AetherSDR

> **Claude Code: read [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) for the full project guide.**
> This file exists only because Claude Code auto-loads `CLAUDE.md`.
> Everything project-wide — architecture, conventions, the constitution,
> commit signing, branch protection rules, the must-knows — lives in
> `AGENTS.md` so every AI tool reads the same source of truth.

## Why this is a thin file

AetherSDR's contribution surface is multi-agent in practice (six+
distinct AI tools touch the codebase). Each tool has its own
well-known file at a different path:

| Tool | File |
|---|---|
| Claude Code | `CLAUDE.md` (you are here) |
| OpenAI Codex CLI / spec-kit / Foundry | `AGENTS.md` (the canonical) |
| GitHub Copilot | `.github/copilot-instructions.md` |
| Gemini Code Assist | `GEMINI.md` |
| Aider | `CONVENTIONS.md` |

All of those are pointers to `AGENTS.md`. The duplication-by-pointer
pattern keeps each tool reading from its native discovery path without
forcing a single canonical file to be copied N times.

## Claude-Code-specific notes

These tidbits are about how Claude Code as a tool interacts with this
codebase. Everything else is in `AGENTS.md`.

- **`git ship` alias** — the project has a `git ship` shell alias
  documented in `AGENTS.md` that squashes local commits ahead of
  `origin/main`, creates a branch, pushes, opens a PR with
  auto-squash-merge enabled. Use it for batch shipping rather than
  one-commit-per-PR.
- **Skills directory** — Claude Code's skill loader picks up skills
  from `~/.claude/skills/`. The AetherClaude bot keeps its own copies
  under `~/build/aetherclaude/skills/`. Those are the authoritative
  source for any agent-orchestration logic.
- **Plans directory** — implementation plans live at
  `~/.claude/plans/`. They are conversation-scoped scratch files,
  not project-canon; `AGENTS.md`, `CONSTITUTION.md`, and
  `CONTRIBUTING.md` are.
- **Memory directory** — Claude Code's auto-memory lives at
  `~/.claude/projects/-home-jeremy-build-AetherSDR/memory/`. Index
  is `MEMORY.md`. Use it for cross-session continuity; everything in
  it is operator-scoped (per Principle XIII), not project-canon.

That's everything Claude-Code-specific. Read `AGENTS.md` for the rest.

name: Sanitizers

# Weekly debug-heavy build that runs the test suite under AddressSanitizer +
# UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer (one matrix entry) and ThreadSanitizer (separate
# entry, incompatible with ASan). Failures are uploaded as artifacts and
# tracked in a sticky GitHub issue per sanitizer kind so the queue does not
# fill with duplicates.

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 7 * * 1'  # Mondays 07:00 UTC
  workflow_dispatch:

permissions:
  contents: read
  issues: write

jobs:
  sanitize:
    name: ${{ matrix.sanitizer.label }}
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    container: ghcr.io/ten9876/aethersdr-ci:latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        sanitizer:
          - id: asan-ubsan
            label: ASan + UBSan
            build_type: Debug
            # ASan + UBSan applied via CXXFLAGS / LDFLAGS so the flags
            # reach every target the build produces — including the
            # standalone unit-test executables (CMakeLists.txt:1273+),
            # which are NOT linked against the AetherSDR target and
            # therefore don't inherit the per-target -fsanitize=address
            # option set at CMakeLists.txt:1260. Setting the flags here
            # also lets us drop that coupling in a future
            # -DAETHERSDR_SANITIZER=... cleanup without touching this
            # workflow.
            extra_cxx: "-fsanitize=address,undefined -fno-sanitize-recover=undefined -fno-omit-frame-pointer"
            extra_ld:  "-fsanitize=address,undefined"

content.content:
### VIII. Evidence Over Assertion

A fix claim is verified by CI and behavior, not by the implementing
agent's confidence. Adopted from
[Foundry I](https://github.com/CiscoDevNet/foundry-security-spec/blob/main/constitution.md#i-evidence-over-assertion).

No agent — Claude Code, AetherClaude, Codex, Copilot, or any other —
may merge or recommend merging a PR on the strength of its own
prose. The verification is `bin/validate-diff.sh` plus CI green
(build, check-paths, check-windows) plus the functional check the
PR's description claims will pass. A claim whose verification step
was not actually run is demoted to a hypothesis, regardless of how
confident the description reads.

*Why this is inviolable: frontier models produce fluent, confident,
plausible fix descriptions that turn out wrong at a rate that makes
unreviewed AI-generated commits dangerous. We have shipped at least
one example this cycle: the `RNNoiseFilter::process()` integration
in PR #2816 was committed with the claim "alloc-free after first
call" — the claim was confident, the test plan was thorough, AetherClaude
specifically challenged it in triage, and the verification step had
to actually be added (test 7) before the claim was credible. Every
attempt to skip the verification gate has surfaced a regression in
production.*

### XI. Fixes Are Demonstrated

A "Fixes #NNNN" claim is the implementing agent's hypothesis; the
squash-merge process is its independent verification. Adopted from
[Foundry VII](https://github.com/CiscoDevNet/foundry-security-spec/blob/main/constitution.md#vii-exploited-means-demonstrated)
and reframed: "exploited" is Foundry's verification trigger for
vulnerabilities; "fixed" is ours for bugs.

A fix is demonstrated by (a) CI re-running on the squash-merge
commit on `main`, NOT on the PR branch, with all three required
checks green; (b) the maintainer reproducing the original bug on
the pre-merge build and confirming it no longer reproduces on the
post-merge build, when the bug is testable; (c) for user-reported
regressions, the original reporter confirming the fix when feasible.
Agent self-grading ("my test plan passed") does not substitute for
the independent re-run.

*Why this is inviolable: the implementing agent has every incentive
to declare its work complete. The squash-merge CI is a separate
process re-running on a separate commit (the squash result, not the
PR head) — that's the independent check. We have used this gate to
catch real regressions: when PR #2797 (the v26.5.2 release commit)
had the same code as the PR but a different SHA, main CI ran
independently and validated the squash result. Skipping the
post-merge CI re-run because "PR CI was green on identical content"
is the rationalization shape that leads to bypassed verification.*

A fix is demonstrated by (a) CI re-running on the squash-merge
commit on `main`, NOT on the PR branch, with all three required
checks green; (b) the maintainer reproducing the original bug on
the pre-merge build and confirming it no longer reproduces on the
post-merge build, when the bug is testable; (c) for user-reported
regressions, the original reporter confirming the fix when feasible.
Agent self-grading ("my test plan passed") does not substitute for
the independent re-run.

*Why this is inviolable: the implementing agent has every incentive
to declare its work complete. The squash-merge CI is a separate
process re-running on a separate commit (the squash result, not the
PR head) — that's the independent check. We have used this gate to
catch real regressions: when PR #2797 (the v26.5.2 release commit)
had the same code as the PR but a different SHA, main CI ran
independently and validated the squash result. Skipping the
post-merge CI re-run because "PR CI was green on identical content"
is the rationalization shape that leads to bypassed verification.*
